title: Web Fundamentals + HTML + CSS + JavaScript Engine

weeks.title: Week 1: How the Web Works: HTTP, DNS, Client/Server, Browsers

days.title: Monday: Week 1 ┬╖ HTTP/HTTPS Model + Dev Environment
tasks: | ЁЯЫа **Technologies** | Node.js 22 LTS, VS Code, pnpm, `curl`, `dig`, `openssl` |
| ЁЯУЦ **Concepts** | HTTP methods (GET/POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE), status codes, request/response headers, HTTP/2 multiplexing, TLS handshake, HTTPS vs HTTP |
| ЁЯОп **You Build** | Dev environment configured. `curl -v` dissection of 5 real sites documented. FleetPulse domain registered on Cloudflare. |
| ЁЯФЧ **Why It Matters** | Every web request you ever debug starts with understanding what bytes go over the wire. Without this, networking errors are magic. |

**Morning тАФ HTTP from First Principles**
Open your terminal and run `curl -v https://google.com`. You will see the exact bytes exchanged:
- **TCP handshake** тАФ SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK тАФ before any HTTP data moves
- **TLS handshake** тАФ certificate exchange, cipher negotiation, session key establishment тАФ this is what HTTPS adds on top of HTTP
- **HTTP request** тАФ request line (`GET / HTTP/1.1`), headers (`Host:`, `User-Agent:`, `Accept-Encoding:`), blank line, optional body

- **HTTP response** тАФ status line (`HTTP/1.1 301 Moved Permanently`), response headers, blank line, body
HTTP status code families you must know cold:
- **2xx** тАФ success: 200 OK, 201 Created, 204 No Content
- **3xx** тАФ redirect: 301 Permanent, 302 Temporary, 304 Not Modified (cached)
- **4xx** тАФ client error: 400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden, 404 Not Found, 409 Conflict, 422 Unprocessable, 429 Too Many Requests

- **5xx** тАФ server error: 500 Internal Server Error, 502 Bad Gateway, 503 Service Unavailable, 504 Gateway Timeout
**HTTP/2**: multiplexes multiple requests over a single TCP connection. HTTP/1.1 required one request at a time per connection, causing head-of-line blocking. HTTP/2 sends requests and responses interleaved as numbered frames. Browsers open one connection to a domain instead of 6тАУ8 in parallel.
**HTTPS vs HTTP**: HTTP is plaintext тАФ anyone on the network can read it. HTTPS wraps HTTP in TLS: data is encrypted, the server's identity is verified via certificate, and data integrity is guaranteed. The TLS handshake adds ~1 round trip of latency (TLS 1.3 reduces this).
**VS Code Setup**: install ESLint, Prettier, Error Lens, GitLens, Thunder Client. Configure format-on-save. Set up pnpm workspaces with `packages/types`, `packages/schemas`, `packages/utils`.

**DSA тАФ Big O Notation**
Review O(1), O(log N), O(N), O(N log N), O(N┬▓). Connect to what you just learned: HTTP header lookup is O(1) (hash map). DNS recursive resolution is O(depth of tree). Sorting HTTP logs is O(N log N).

days.title: Tuesday: Week 1 ┬╖ Client/Server Concepts + REST Design
tasks: | ЁЯЫа **Technologies** | Node.js `http` stdlib, `curl`, `httpie` |
| ЁЯУЦ **Concepts** | Stateless vs stateful, REST resource design, idempotency (GET/PUT/DELETE vs POST), request/response lifecycle, connection keep-alive |
| ЁЯОп **You Build** | FleetPulse raw GPS receiver тАФ plain `http.createServer`, no framework. Handles malformed requests gracefully. |
| ЁЯФЧ **Why It Matters** | REST is not a library тАФ it is a set of constraints. Understanding them means you design APIs that are predictable and debuggable. |

**Morning тАФ Stateless Architecture and REST**
A **stateless** server does not remember who you are between requests. Every request carries all the information needed to fulfill it (auth token, session ID, all required data). This is why HTTP scales horizontally тАФ any server in the cluster can handle any request because no server holds your session.
**REST** (Representational State Transfer) has six constraints. The important ones:
- **Uniform interface** тАФ resources identified by URLs, manipulated through representations, stateless interactions
- **Statelessness** тАФ no session state on the server

- **Cacheability** тАФ responses must label themselves as cacheable or not
REST URL design rules:
- Nouns, not verbs: `/drivers`, not `/getDrivers`
- Plural: `/drivers/123`, not `/driver/123`
- Hierarchy represents relationships: `/drivers/123/trips`

- Query strings for filtering: `/trips?status=pending&driverId=123`
**Idempotency**: an operation is idempotent if applying it multiple times produces the same result as applying it once. GET, PUT, DELETE are idempotent. POST is not. This matters enormously for retries тАФ a network error on a payment POST could cause a double charge if the server processed it but the response was lost.

**Evening тАФ Build the FleetPulse GPS Receiver**
Build a raw HTTP server using only `http.createServer`:
- `POST /pings` тАФ accepts GPS ping JSON, validates it, responds `{ok: true}`
- `GET /health` тАФ health check endpoint
- Handle body chunking manually тАФ `req.on('data')` and `req.on('end')`
- Handle all error cases: malformed JSON, missing fields, wrong content-type

- Log every request: method, path, status, duration in milliseconds
Why no Express yet? Express is just a pretty wrapper. Understanding the underlying `http` module means you can debug Express internals, write custom middleware, and understand every framework that runs on Node.js.

**DSA тАФ Array and HashMap Problems**
Two Sum with HashMap O(N). Valid Anagram with character frequency map. Connection: the HashMap pattern (trade space for time) is the foundation of Redis, database indexes, and DNS caches.

days.title: Wednesday: Week 1 ┬╖ DNS тАФ From Browser to IP
tasks: | ЁЯЫа **Technologies** | `dig`, `nslookup`, Cloudflare DNS, Wireshark |
| ЁЯУЦ **Concepts** | DNS record types (A, CNAME, MX, TXT, NS, PTR), TTL, recursive vs iterative resolution, DNS caching at every level, CDN DNS routing |
| ЁЯОп **You Build** | Register `fleetpulse.dev`, `bookwise.dev`, `routemaster.dev`, `paycoreapp.dev` on Cloudflare. Configure A and CNAME records. Set TTLs. |
| ЁЯФЧ **Why It Matters** | Every time you deploy, DNS propagation delays can confuse your users. Understanding TTL means you know when to change it (lower TTL before a migration, raise it after). |

**Morning тАФ How DNS Resolution Works**
Run `dig +trace google.com` and follow every step:
1. **Browser cache** тАФ recently resolved names are cached (TTL seconds)
2. **OS cache** тАФ `/etc/hosts` and OS resolver cache checked first
3. **Recursive resolver** тАФ usually your ISP or `8.8.8.8` (Google) or `1.1.1.1` (Cloudflare)
4. **Root servers** тАФ 13 root server addresses, hardcoded in resolvers. They know where `.com` TLD servers are.
5. **TLD servers** тАФ `.com` servers know where `google.com`'s nameservers are
6. **Authoritative nameservers** тАФ Google's own nameservers return the final A record
DNS record types:
- **A** тАФ maps hostname to IPv4 address: `fleetpulse.dev тЖТ 1.2.3.4`
- **AAAA** тАФ maps hostname to IPv6 address
- **CNAME** тАФ alias to another hostname: `www.fleetpulse.dev тЖТ fleetpulse.dev`
- **MX** тАФ mail server for the domain: `fleetpulse.dev тЖТ mail.fleetpulse.dev`
- **TXT** тАФ arbitrary text: used for SPF/DKIM email authentication, domain ownership verification
- **NS** тАФ nameserver records тАФ which servers are authoritative for this domain

- **PTR** тАФ reverse DNS: IP address тЖТ hostname (used by email servers for spam checks)
**TTL (Time To Live)**: how long resolvers cache the answer. Low TTL (60 seconds) = changes propagate quickly but more DNS traffic. High TTL (86400 = 1 day) = caches everywhere, changes take 24 hours to propagate. Rule: lower your TTL 48 hours before any planned migration, raise it back after.

days.title: Thursday: Week 1 ┬╖ How Websites Work тАФ Browser Rendering Pipeline
tasks: | ЁЯЫа **Technologies** | Chrome DevTools (Elements, Network, Performance, Lighthouse), `curl` |
| ЁЯУЦ **Concepts** | HTML тЖТ DOM тЖТ CSSOM тЖТ Render Tree тЖТ Layout тЖТ Paint тЖТ Composite, critical render path, reflow vs repaint, Lighthouse scoring |
| ЁЯОп **You Build** | Profile FleetPulse landing page with Lighthouse. Document every bottleneck. Achieve 90+ score before adding any React. |
| ЁЯФЧ **Why It Matters** | Framework performance problems start here. Understanding the render pipeline means you know why React.memo, CSS containment, and lazy loading exist. |

**Morning тАФ The Browser Rendering Pipeline** When a browser receives HTML, it does not just display it. It executes a complex pipeline: 1. **Parse HTML тЖТ DOM** тАФ the browser parses HTML into a tree of nodes (Document Object Model). `
`, `

`, `` all become nodes. 2. **Parse CSS тЖТ CSSOM** тАФ CSS is parsed into a separate tree (CSS Object Model). Stylesheets block rendering тАФ the browser must finish downloading and parsing all CSS before it can paint. 3. **Combine тЖТ Render Tree** тАФ DOM + CSSOM = Render Tree. Only visible nodes are included (`display: none` nodes are excluded). 4. **Layout (Reflow)** тАФ the browser calculates the exact position and size of every element on screen. This is expensive. Changing any property that affects layout (width, height, top, left, margin) triggers a reflow of potentially the entire document. 5. **Paint** тАФ fills in pixels for each element: text, colors, images, shadows. Changing `color` or `background-color` triggers repaint but not reflow (cheaper). 6. **Composite** тАФ some elements (those with `transform`, `opacity`, `will-change`) are painted on separate GPU layers and composited together. Animating `transform` and `opacity` only triggers compositing тАФ the cheapest operation. This is why CSS animations using `transform` are smooth and animations using `top/left` are janky.
**Chrome DevTools mastery**:
- **Elements tab** тАФ inspect and modify the live DOM. See which CSS rules apply, computed styles, box model dimensions.
- **Network tab** тАФ every request: timing (DNS, TCP, TLS, TTFB, download), headers, response body. Waterfall view shows parallel vs sequential loading.
- **Performance tab** тАФ record a session, see flame chart of JS execution, layout events, paint events, FPS.
- **Lighthouse** тАФ automated audit: Performance (LCP, CLS, FID), Accessibility (ARIA, color contrast), Best Practices (HTTPS, console errors), SEO (meta tags, robots.txt).

**DSA тАФ Stack and Queue**
Implement both with arrays and linked lists. Connection: the browser's rendering pipeline is a queue. The JavaScript call stack is a stack. You will use both data structures in your scheduler utility.

days.title: Friday: Week 1 ┬╖ CLI + Shell Scripting
tasks: | ЁЯЫа **Technologies** | Bash, `zsh`, `grep`, `sed`, `awk`, `find`, `top`, `htop`, `ps`, `kill`, `cron` |
| ЁЯУЦ **Concepts** | File system hierarchy, pipes and redirection, process management, environment variables, shell scripts, cron jobs |
| ЁЯОп **You Build** | Deploy script for FleetPulse: stops old server, pulls new code, runs tests, starts new server, health checks. |
| ЁЯФЧ **Why It Matters** | Every DevOps operation, every production debug session, every CI pipeline is shell commands. You cannot be a backend engineer without owning the command line. |

**Morning тАФ The Command Line**
Essential commands organized by category:
**File system**: `ls -la` (list with permissions), `find . -name "*.ts" -mtime -1` (files modified in last day), `cp -r src dst` (recursive copy), `mv`, `rm -rf`, `mkdir -p path/to/dir` (create with parents), `chmod 755 script.sh`, `chown user:group file`
**Text processing**: `grep -r "TODO" src/` (search recursively), `grep -v "test" file` (exclude matches), `sed 's/old/new/g' file` (replace in file), `awk '{print $1, $3}' file` (print columns 1 and 3), `sort | uniq -c | sort -nr` (count occurrences, sort by count)
**Pipes and redirection**: `command | command` (pipe stdout to stdin), `command > file` (redirect stdout to file), `command >> file` (append), `command 2>&1 | tee file` (capture both stdout and stderr, also print to terminal)
**Process management**: `ps aux | grep node` (find Node.js processes), `kill -9 PID` (force kill), `top` / `htop` (live process view), `lsof -i :3000` (what is using port 3000), `ulimit -n 65536` (raise file descriptor limit), `nohup command &` (run in background, persist after logout)

**Environment**: `export KEY=value`, `printenv`, `source .env`, `.env` files loaded with `dotenv`
**Cron**: `crontab -e` opens the cron editor. Format: `minute hour day month weekday command`. `0 2 * * * /home/ubuntu/backup.sh` runs at 2am daily.

**Evening тАФ Write the Deploy Script**
Write a shell script that: checks Node.js version, runs `pnpm test`, builds the app, copies files to the server via rsync, SSHs in and runs the restart commands, polls the health endpoint until it returns 200. This script will be used every day for the rest of the plan.

weeks.title: Week 2: HTML + CSS: Semantic Markup, Box Model, Flexbox, Grid, Accessibility

days.title: Monday: Week 2 ┬╖ HTML тАФ Semantic Markup + Accessibility + Forms
tasks: | ЁЯЫа **Technologies** | HTML5, browser DevTools, WAVE (accessibility checker), Lighthouse |
| ЁЯУЦ **Concepts** | Semantic HTML elements, ARIA attributes, form elements and validation, `<head>` structure, meta tags, accessibility tree |
| ЁЯОп **You Build** | FleetPulse driver dashboard markup тАФ semantic HTML with no CSS yet. Passes WAVE accessibility check. |

| ЁЯФЧ **Why It Matters** | Screen readers and search engines read your HTML, not your CSS. Semantic HTML is free SEO and accessibility. React Testing Library's `getByRole` works because of semantic HTML. |

**Morning тАФ HTML Done Right**
HTML has 100+ elements. Most developers use 10. The rest provide **semantic meaning** тАФ they tell browsers, search engines, and screen readers what role each piece of content plays.
Structural semantic elements:
- `<header>` тАФ introductory content, logo, navigation
- `<nav>` тАФ navigation links
- `<main>` тАФ the primary content of the page (only one per page)
- `<article>` тАФ self-contained content: a blog post, a product card, a news story
- `<section>` тАФ thematic grouping within a document, with a heading
- `<aside>` тАФ tangentially related content: sidebars, related links

- `<footer>` тАФ footer information: copyright, links, contact
Why use these instead of `<div>` everywhere? Search engines weight content in `<main>` and `<article>` more heavily. Screen readers announce these landmarks so users can jump to them. `getByRole('main')` in tests only works with proper semantics.
**ARIA (Accessible Rich Internet Applications)**: fills semantic gaps. `role="dialog"` marks a modal. `aria-label="Close"` names an icon button with no text. `aria-expanded={isOpen}` communicates dropdown state. `aria-live="polite"` announces dynamic content changes to screen readers.
**Forms done right**:
- `<label for="email">` linked to `<input id="email">` тАФ clicking the label focuses the input
- `required`, `pattern`, `minlength`, `type="email"` тАФ native browser validation before JavaScript runs
- `<fieldset>` + `<legend>` тАФ group related fields
- `<button type="submit">` not `<div onclick>` тАФ keyboard accessible by default

days.title: Tuesday: Week 2 ┬╖ CSS тАФ Box Model, Cascade, Specificity, Custom Properties
tasks: | ЁЯЫа **Technologies** | CSS3, Chrome DevTools Computed tab |
| ЁЯУЦ **Concepts** | Box model (content, padding, border, margin), `box-sizing: border-box`, cascade algorithm, specificity scoring, inheritance, CSS custom properties |
| ЁЯОп **You Build** | FleetPulse layout styled with pure CSS тАФ no framework. Box model understood by measuring every element in DevTools. |
| ЁЯФЧ **Why It Matters** | Tailwind generates CSS. If you do not understand CSS, you cannot debug when Tailwind produces unexpected results. |

**Morning тАФ The CSS Box Model**
Every HTML element is a rectangular box. The box has four layers from inside out:
1. **Content** тАФ where text and images render
2. **Padding** тАФ transparent space inside the border
3. **Border** тАФ the border itself (can have width, style, color)
4. **Margin** тАФ transparent space outside the border (collapses with adjacent margins)
`box-sizing: content-box` (default): `width` = content width only. Padding and border add to total size. A 200px wide box with 20px padding is 240px total. **This is confusing.**
`box-sizing: border-box`: `width` = total size including padding and border. A 200px wide box with 20px padding has 160px of content space. **Always use this.** Add `*, *::before, *::after { box-sizing: border-box }` to every project.
**The Cascade**: when multiple CSS rules target the same element, the cascade determines which wins:
1. Specificity тАФ more specific selector wins
2. Source order тАФ later rule wins if specificity is equal
3. `!important` тАФ overrides everything (avoid it)
**Specificity scoring**: `inline style (1000) > ID (100) > class/attribute/pseudo-class (10) > element (1)`. `.nav .link` scores 20. `#header` scores 100. `div p a` scores 3.

days.title: Wednesday: Week 2 ┬╖ CSS Flexbox + Grid + Responsive Design
tasks: | ЁЯЫа **Technologies** | CSS Flexbox, CSS Grid, media queries, `clamp()` |
| ЁЯУЦ **Concepts** | Flex container vs flex items, main axis vs cross axis, Grid template areas, auto-fill vs auto-fit, intrinsic vs extrinsic sizing, mobile-first design |
| ЁЯОп **You Build** | FleetPulse driver grid layout тАФ 1 column on mobile, 2 on tablet, 3 on desktop. No media query hacks. |
| ЁЯФЧ **Why It Matters** | Flexbox and Grid replaced float-based and table-based layouts. Understanding them means you understand what Tailwind's `flex`, `grid`, `col-span` classes actually do. |

**Morning тАФ Flexbox**
Flexbox solves one-dimensional layout: a row or column of items.
Key flex container properties:
- `display: flex` тАФ creates a flex container. Direct children become flex items.
- `flex-direction: row | column` тАФ main axis direction
- `justify-content` тАФ alignment along the main axis: `flex-start`, `center`, `space-between`, `space-around`
- `align-items` тАФ alignment along the cross axis: `flex-start`, `center`, `stretch`
- `gap` тАФ space between items (no margin needed)

- `flex-wrap: wrap` тАФ items wrap to next line when they overflow
Key flex item properties:
- `flex: 1` тАФ shorthand for `flex-grow: 1; flex-shrink: 1; flex-basis: 0`. Items grow to fill available space equally.
- `flex: 0 0 200px` тАФ fixed 200px width, no grow, no shrink

**Responsive design with `clamp()`**:
```css
font-size: clamp(1rem, 2.5vw, 2rem);
```
Minimum 1rem, maximum 2rem, scales with viewport width. No breakpoints needed for typography.

days.title: Thursday: Week 2 ┬╖ CSS Animations + Transitions + Tailwind CSS
tasks: | ЁЯЫа **Technologies** | CSS animations, `@keyframes`, CSS transitions, Tailwind CSS |
| ЁЯУЦ **Concepts** | Transition properties, `transform` vs layout properties (GPU layer), `will-change`, `@keyframes`, animation compositing, Tailwind's utility-first model |
| ЁЯОп **You Build** | FleetPulse UI rebuilt with Tailwind. All custom CSS deleted. `cn()` and `cva()` utility for conditional classes. |

| ЁЯФЧ **Why It Matters** | Animating `transform` and `opacity` uses the GPU compositor тАФ 60fps. Animating `top/left/width/height` causes reflow тАФ janky. This is why Motion/Framer Motion only animates transform and opacity by default. |

days.title: Friday: Week 2 ┬╖ Shadcn UI + Radix UI + Component Systems
tasks: | ЁЯЫа **Technologies** | Shadcn UI, Radix UI, Tailwind |
| ЁЯУЦ **Concepts** | Headless components, compound component pattern, controlled vs uncontrolled, accessibility built-in (keyboard navigation, ARIA), owning your component source |
| ЁЯОп **You Build** | FleetPulse UI uses Shadcn: DataTable for driver list, Dialog for trip detail, Command palette for search |

| ЁЯФЧ **Why It Matters** | Radix UI provides the accessibility and keyboard navigation logic. Tailwind provides the styling. Shadcn combines both and puts the code in your repo тАФ you own it, you can modify it. |

**Morning тАФ Headless Component Architecture**
A **headless component** provides behavior and accessibility with no opinion on visual appearance. Radix UI's `<Dialog.Root>`, `<Dialog.Overlay>`, `<Dialog.Content>` handle:
- Focus trap when dialog opens (keyboard users cannot tab outside)
- `Escape` key closes the dialog
- `aria-modal`, `role="dialog"`, `aria-labelledby` automatically set

- Click outside to close
Shadcn takes Radix primitives and wraps them in Tailwind-styled components. You run `npx shadcn-ui@latest add button` and the **source code is copied into your project** at `components/ui/button.tsx`. You own the code. You can modify every pixel without forking a library.
This is profoundly different from a component library like MUI тАФ with Shadcn, there is no black box, no version lock-in, no `!important` fights.

days.title: WeekendCapstone тАФ Web Fundamentals Complete
tasks: All 5 platform landing pages have: semantic HTML (passes WAVE), Tailwind CSS with Shadcn components, Lighthouse 90+ score, deployed to Cloudflare Pages. DNS configured. Deploy script working.

weeks.title: Week 3: JavaScript Engine: Types, Scope, Closures, Prototypes, `this`

days.title: Monday: Week 3 ┬╖ JavaScript Types + Coercion + VS Code Setup
tasks: | ЁЯЫа **Technologies** | Node.js 22 LTS, VS Code with ESLint + Prettier + Error Lens, TypeScript |
| ЁЯУЦ **Concepts** | Primitive vs reference types, `typeof`, `===` vs `==`, truthy/falsy, `null` vs `undefined` vs `NaN`, type coercion rules |
| ЁЯОп **You Build** | `packages/utils/validate.ts` тАФ GPS coordinate validator using strict type checks. 20 Vitest tests for edge cases. |
| ЁЯФЧ **Why It Matters** | Type coercion bugs are silent. `"5" == 5` is true. `[] == false` is true. `null == undefined` is true but `null === undefined` is false. These cause production bugs. |

**Morning тАФ JavaScript Types in Depth**
JavaScript has 8 types. 7 are **primitives** (stored by value, immutable): `string`, `number`, `bigint`, `boolean`, `null`, `undefined`, `symbol`. 1 is a **reference type**: `object` (arrays, functions, and all objects are objects under the hood).
```javascript
typeof "hello"     // "string"
typeof 42          // "number"
typeof true        // "boolean"
typeof undefined   // "undefined"
typeof null        // "object" тЖР famous bug in the spec, never fixed for backwards compatibility
typeof []          // "object"
typeof function(){} // "function"
```
Truthy/falsy тАФ values that evaluate to false in boolean context: `false`, `0`, `""`, `null`, `undefined`, `NaN`. Everything else is truthy. Surprise: `[]` is truthy. `{}` is truthy. `"0"` is truthy.
`NaN`: Not a Number. `typeof NaN === "number"` (another spec quirk). `NaN !== NaN` тАФ NaN is not equal to itself. Use `Number.isNaN(x)` not `x === NaN` to check.
**ESLint + Prettier Setup**:
- ESLint catches real bugs: undefined variables, unused imports, unsafe coercions
- Prettier formats code (indentation, quotes, semicolons) consistently across the entire team
- Error Lens shows lint errors inline in VS Code without opening the Problems panel
- Configure `eslint` with TypeScript rules: `@typescript-eslint/no-explicit-any`, `@typescript-eslint/no-floating-promises`

days.title: Tuesday: Week 3 ┬╖ Scope + Closures + `var`/`let`/`const`
tasks: | ЁЯЫа **Technologies** | Node.js, TypeScript, Vitest |
| ЁЯУЦ **Concepts** | Lexical scope, closure mechanics, hoisting, Temporal Dead Zone, IIFE, module scope |
| ЁЯОп **You Build** | `packages/utils/retry.ts` тАФ exponential backoff with jitter, closure over config. 8 Vitest tests. |
| ЁЯФЧ **Why It Matters** | Closures are used in every callback, every React hook, every module. The loop variable bug causes production bugs today in real codebases. |
- **`var`** тАФ function-scoped, hoisted with value `undefined`, can be redeclared. Avoid in new code.
- **`let`** тАФ block-scoped, in Temporal Dead Zone before declaration (accessing = `ReferenceError`)
- **`const`** тАФ block-scoped, binding cannot be reassigned. The object it points to can still be mutated.

days.title: Wednesday: Week 3 ┬╖ Prototypes + `class` + the Four Rules of `this`
tasks: | ЁЯЫа **Technologies** | Node.js, TypeScript, Vitest |
| ЁЯУЦ **Concepts** | Prototype chain, `Object.create`, `class` as syntax sugar, the 4 steps of `new`, 4 rules of `this`, arrow functions and lexical `this` |
| ЁЯОп **You Build** | `packages/utils/emitter.ts` тАФ EventEmitter from scratch using private fields, prototypes, explicit `this` binding |
| ЁЯФЧ **Why It Matters** | Node.js Streams, EventEmitter, and every browser API are built on prototypes. Understanding `this` prevents the most common JavaScript bug category. |

**Morning тАФ JavaScript's Object System**
JavaScript does not have classes in the traditional sense. It has **prototypes**. `class` is syntax sugar тАФ it compiles to the same prototype-based code.
The **prototype chain**: when you access a property on an object and it is not there, JavaScript looks at `object.__proto__`, then `object.__proto__.__proto__`, all the way to `null`. This is inheritance in JavaScript.
The 4 steps of `new Foo()`:
1. Create a new empty object `{}`
2. Set its `__proto__` to `Foo.prototype`
3. Call `Foo` with `this` pointing to the new object
4. Return `this` (or the explicitly returned object if it is an object)
**The 4 rules of `this`** тАФ evaluated in order:
| Priority | Rule | Example |
| 1 (highest) | `new` binding | `new Foo()` тЖТ `this` = new object |
| 2 | Explicit binding | `fn.call(obj)` тЖТ `this` = `obj` |
| 3 | Implicit binding | `obj.fn()` тЖТ `this` = `obj` |

| 4 (lowest) | Default binding | `fn()` тЖТ `this` = `globalThis` (or `undefined` in strict mode) |
**Arrow functions** have no own `this`. They capture `this` from the lexical scope where they are written. This is why you can use `() => this.method()` inside a class without losing the instance reference.

days.title: Thursday: Week 3 ┬╖ Event Loop тАФ The Most Important JavaScript Concept
tasks: | ЁЯЫа **Technologies** | Node.js, Chrome DevTools Performance tab |
| ЁЯУЦ **Concepts** | Call stack, Web APIs/libuv, macrotask queue, microtask queue, `process.nextTick`, `setImmediate` ordering |
| ЁЯОп **You Build** | `packages/utils/scheduler.ts` тАФ priority task scheduler using min-heap. 20 execution-order puzzles predicted correctly. |
| ЁЯФЧ **Why It Matters** | Every performance bug, every mysterious callback ordering, every "why did my code run in the wrong order" question is an event loop question. |

**Morning тАФ The Event Loop, Step by Step**
JavaScript is single-threaded: it executes one thing at a time. The event loop coordinates what runs next.
Three areas:
**Call Stack**: synchronous code. When you call a function, it is pushed. When it returns, it is popped. When the stack is empty, the event loop picks the next task.
**Web APIs / libuv**: async operations. `setTimeout(cb, 0)` registers `cb` with the timer API. When the timer fires, `cb` is placed in the macrotask queue. `fetch()` is handled by libuv. `fs.readFile` is handled by a thread pool.
**Queues**:
- **Microtask queue** тАФ `Promise.then`, `queueMicrotask`, `MutationObserver`. **Drained completely before the next macrotask.**

days.title: Friday: Week 3 ┬╖ Promises + `async/await` + Generators
tasks: | ЁЯЫа **Technologies** | Node.js, TypeScript, Vitest |

- **`Promise.all`** тАФ wait for all; fail fast on first rejection
- **`Promise.allSettled`** тАФ wait for all; collect all results regardless of success/failure
- **`Promise.race`** тАФ first to settle wins (use for timeouts)

- **`Promise.any`** тАФ first to fulfill wins; only rejects if all reject
**Generators**: a `function*` can `yield` values one at a time. Execution pauses at `yield` and resumes when `.next()` is called. Used for: lazy infinite sequences, custom iterables, `for...of` loops over async data.

days.title: Monday: Week 4 ┬╖ CommonJS vs ESM + Functional Patterns
tasks: | ЁЯЫа **Technologies** | Node.js (`require`/`import`), TypeScript, Vitest |

**Morning тАФ Module Systems**
**CommonJS** (`require`): synchronous тАФ `require()` blocks until the file loads. Modules are cached after first load. Circular dependencies return a partially-constructed object (the part that ran before the cycle was hit).
**ESM** (`import`/`export`): static тАФ the import graph is analyzed before any code runs. This enables tree shaking: if you import `{ formatDate }` from a utility library and `formatDate` has no side effects, the bundler can exclude all other exports from the bundle. Dynamic `import()` is asynchronous тАФ it returns a Promise.
**Functional patterns** тАФ implement from scratch, then use built-ins:
- `map` = transform each element (like a SELECT in SQL)
- `filter` = keep matching elements (like a WHERE in SQL)
- `reduce` = collapse to a value (like an aggregation in SQL)
- `pipe(f, g, h)(x)` = `h(g(f(x)))` тАФ left to right
- `curry(add)(2)(3)` = `add(2, 3)` тАФ enables partial application

weeks.title: Week 5: Node.js Internals: V8, libuv, Streams, `worker_threads`

days.title: Monday: Week 5 ┬╖ V8 Architecture тАФ How Node.js Runs Your Code
tasks: | ЁЯЫа **Technologies** | `node --inspect`, Chrome DevTools Memory/Performance tabs |
| ЁЯУЦ **Concepts** | JIT pipeline (Ignition bytecode тЖТ TurboFan native code), hidden classes, inline caching, generational GC (young/old generation), GC pauses |
| ЁЯОп **You Build** | Flame graph analysis of two function versions тАФ consistent vs inconsistent property order. Document the speedup from fixing hidden class deoptimization. |
| ЁЯФЧ **Why It Matters** | Hidden class deoptimization can silently make a function 10x slower. Knowing this lets you write high-performance object-heavy code. |

**Concepts тАФ Inside V8**
V8's JIT pipeline:
1. **Parser** тЖТ source code тЖТ AST (Abstract Syntax Tree)
2. **Ignition** тЖТ AST тЖТ bytecode (fast to generate, interpreted immediately)
3. **TurboFan** тЖТ hot bytecode тЖТ optimized native machine code
**Hidden classes**: V8 assigns a hidden class to every object based on its shape (which properties exist, in what order). Objects with identical shapes share a hidden class, allowing V8 to generate optimized property access. Adding properties in different orders creates different hidden classes тАФ V8 must deoptimize and fall back to slower dictionary-based access.
**Generational GC**: Objects are born in the **young generation** (nursery) тАФ collected frequently with a fast scavenge algorithm. Objects that survive 2 collections are promoted to the **old generation** тАФ collected with a slower mark-and-sweep that causes a "stop the world" pause. Allocating many short-lived objects in hot paths creates GC pressure тАФ measure it with `v8.getHeapStatistics()`.

days.title: Tuesday: Week 5 ┬╖ Streams: Backpressure + `pipeline()`
tasks: | ЁЯЫа **Technologies** | Node.js `stream/promises`, `Transform`, `Writable`, `Readable`, `bufio` |
| ЁЯУЦ **Concepts** | Push vs pull mode, `highWaterMark`, `drain` event, backpressure, `pipeline()` error propagation, `stream.compose()` |
| ЁЯОп **You Build** | FleetPulse GPS log pipeline тАФ 200MB file processed with 20MB constant RAM. Multi-stage: read тЖТ validate тЖТ enrich тЖТ batch-insert PostgreSQL. |
| ЁЯФЧ **Why It Matters** | Without backpressure handling, a fast readable overfills memory and crashes. Every data pipeline, every file import endpoint, every ETL process needs streams. |

**Concepts**
Without streams: `fs.readFile('200mb.jsonl', callback)` тАФ 200MB allocated in memory before processing begins.
With streams: data flows in 64KB chunks by default. Each chunk is processed and garbage collected. Memory usage is constant.
**Backpressure mechanism**: `writable.write(chunk)` returns `false` when the internal buffer exceeds `highWaterMark`. This is the signal to stop reading. Resume on the `'drain'` event. `pipeline()` handles this automatically тАФ it also destroys all streams in the chain when any one errors, preventing resource leaks.
Multi-stage pipeline for FleetPulse:
```javascript
await pipeline(
  fs.createReadStream('gps_events.jsonl'),
  new SplitLinesTransform(),       // chunk тЖТ lines
  new ValidateTransform(),          // parse + Zod validate
  new EnrichWithZoneTransform(),    // add zone data
  new PostgresBatchWritable(db, 500) // bulk insert every 500 rows
);
// memory stays < 25MB for any file size
```

days.title: Wednesday: Week 5 ┬╖ Event Loop Phases Deep + `worker_threads` + `cluster`
tasks: | ЁЯЫа **Technologies** | Node.js `worker_threads`, `cluster`, `SharedArrayBuffer`, `Atomics` |
| ЁЯУЦ **Concepts** | All 6 event loop phases in order, `process.nextTick` placement, `worker_threads` with `SharedArrayBuffer`, `cluster` module for multi-CPU |
| ЁЯОп **You Build** | Benchmark: bcrypt hashing single-thread vs 4 worker threads. 4x speedup documented. |
| ЁЯФЧ **Why It Matters** | Node.js is single-threaded for JS but can use all CPU cores. Knowing which to use (`worker_threads` for CPU-bound, `cluster` for I/O-bound) is a senior-level decision. |
**The Six Event Loop Phases (in order)**:
| Phase | What runs |
| **Timers** | Expired `setTimeout` / `setInterval` callbacks |
| **Pending callbacks** | I/O error callbacks deferred from previous tick |
| **Idle / prepare** | Internal Node.js only |
| **Poll** | New I/O events. **Node.js waits here** if queue is empty and no timers pending. |
| **Check** | `setImmediate` callbacks |

| **Close callbacks** | `socket.on('close')` etc. |
`process.nextTick` runs **between every phase**, not just at the end. `Promise` microtasks also run between phases (after `nextTick`).
`worker_threads`: each worker has its own V8 instance and event loop тАФ true parallelism. `SharedArrayBuffer` lets workers share memory. `Atomics` provides atomic operations for safe concurrent writes.
`cluster`: forks N copies of the process, each with its own event loop. Shares a port via the OS. Round-robin load balancing between workers. Used for I/O-heavy servers that need more than one Node.js event loop.

days.title: Thursday: Week 5 ┬╖ `crypto`, `net`, `AsyncLocalStorage`
tasks: | ЁЯЫа **Technologies** | Node.js `crypto`, `net`, `AsyncLocalStorage` |
| ЁЯУЦ **Concepts** | HMAC-SHA256 for webhook signing, raw TCP server, `AsyncLocalStorage` for request-scoped context, `Buffer` encodings |

**HMAC Webhook Signing**:
```javascript
const signature = crypto
  .createHmac('sha256', webhookSecret)
  .update(rawBody)
  .digest('hex');
// Send as header: X-Webhook-Signature: sha256=<signature>
// Receiver verifies: compute same signature, compare with timingSafeEqual
```
`crypto.timingSafeEqual`: compare two Buffers in constant time. Regular string comparison (`===`) short-circuits at the first mismatch тАФ this leaks timing information that can be used to brute-force the signature. Always use `timingSafeEqual` for comparing secrets.
**`AsyncLocalStorage`**: stores request-scoped data that propagates through all `async/await` chains without being passed as a parameter.

days.title: Monday: Week 11 ┬╖ React: `UI = f(state)` + All Core Hooks
tasks: | ЁЯЫа **Technologies** | React 18, ReactDOM, React DevTools Profiler |
| ЁЯУЦ **Concepts** | Virtual DOM, reconciliation, fiber, `useState`, `useEffect`, `useRef`, `useCallback`, `useMemo`, `useContext`, `useReducer`, `React.memo` |
| ЁЯОп **You Build** | FleetPulse driver dashboard in React тАФ replaces the vanilla JS version from Week 2. Profiler shows zero unnecessary re-renders. |

| ЁЯФЧ **Why It Matters** | Understanding reconciliation tells you when `React.memo` helps (when props are stable references) and when it hurts (when the memo comparison is more expensive than the render). |
React's model: `UI = f(state)`. Same state тЖТ same UI. State changes тЖТ React re-renders and diffs the new tree against the old (reconciliation). Only actual DOM differences are applied.
Hook reference:
- `useState` тАФ local state; setter schedules re-render
- `useEffect(fn, deps)` тАФ side effect after render; cleanup function on unmount and before next effect
- `useCallback(fn, deps)` тАФ memoize function reference; prevents child re-renders when passing callbacks
- `useMemo(fn, deps)` тАФ memoize computed value; only for genuinely expensive computations
- `useRef` тАФ mutable value that does not trigger re-render; also for DOM references
- `useReducer(reducer, initialState)` тАФ for complex state machines; `dispatch(action)` pattern
- `useContext` тАФ consume context value; every consumer re-renders on any context change

weeks.title: Week 13: Relational Databases Deep + PostgreSQL Mastery

days.title: Monday: Week 13 ┬╖ Relational Databases: ACID + Transactions + Isolation Levels
tasks: | ЁЯЫа **Technologies** | PostgreSQL, `psql`, `EXPLAIN ANALYZE` |
| ЁЯУЦ **Concepts** | ACID properties (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability), all 4 isolation levels with live anomaly demos, `SELECT FOR UPDATE`, `ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE` (upsert) |
| ЁЯОп **You Build** | PayCore double-entry ledger тАФ every financial movement creates two journal entries in a single transaction that are guaranteed to both commit or both roll back. |

| ЁЯФЧ **Why It Matters** | Without ACID transactions, a server crash mid-payment could debit one account without crediting the other. Understanding isolation levels prevents phantom reads and non-repeatable reads in analytics. |
**The 4 Isolation Levels and their anomalies**:
| Level | Dirty Read | Non-Repeatable Read | Phantom Read |
| Read Uncommitted | тЬУ possible | тЬУ possible | тЬУ possible |
| Read Committed (PG default) | тЬЧ prevented | тЬУ possible | тЬУ possible |
| Repeatable Read | тЬЧ prevented | тЬЧ prevented | тЬУ possible |
| Serializable | тЬЧ prevented | тЬЧ prevented | тЬЧ prevented |
**Demonstrate each anomaly live**:
- **Dirty read**: T1 reads uncommitted data from T2. T2 rolls back. T1 acted on data that never existed.
- **Non-repeatable read**: T1 reads a row. T2 updates it. T1 reads again тАФ different value.

- **Phantom read**: T1 reads rows matching a condition. T2 inserts a row matching that condition. T1 reads again тАФ new row appears.
`SELECT FOR UPDATE` тАФ locks the selected rows so no other transaction can modify them until you commit. Used in DungBeetle for atomic job claiming and in PayCore for balance checks.

days.title: Tuesday: Week 13 ┬╖ PostgreSQL Internals: MVCC + WAL + Indexes
tasks: | ЁЯЫа **Technologies** | PostgreSQL, `pg_stat_activity`, `pg_locks`, WAL settings |
| ЁЯУЦ **Concepts** | MVCC (Multi-Version Concurrency Control) тАФ no locks for reads, vacuum, bloat, WAL (Write-Ahead Log) for durability and replication |
| ЁЯОп **You Build** | All 5 platform schemas with proper indexes. `EXPLAIN ANALYZE` on every endpoint тАФ no seq scans on tables > 10K rows. |

| ЁЯФЧ **Why It Matters** | MVCC is why PostgreSQL readers never block writers and writers never block readers. WAL is what makes point-in-time recovery possible. |
**MVCC**: when you update a row, PostgreSQL does not modify it in place. It writes a new version with a transaction ID and marks the old version as deleted. Readers see the version that was current when their transaction started тАФ no matter how many updates happen concurrently. `VACUUM` reclaims space from old versions.
**Index types**:
- **B-tree** (default) тАФ balanced tree, O(log N), used for `=`, `<`, `>`, `BETWEEN`, `ORDER BY`
- **GIN** тАФ inverted index for array contains, full-text search (`tsvector`)
- **GiST** тАФ generalized search tree for geometric types, PostGIS
- **BRIN** тАФ block range index for naturally ordered data (timestamps in append-only tables) тАФ tiny size
- **Partial index** тАФ `CREATE INDEX ON trips(driver_id) WHERE status = 'active'` тАФ tiny, fast for filtered queries
- **Covering index** тАФ `CREATE INDEX ON orders(user_id) INCLUDE (total, status)` тАФ query satisfied entirely from index

days.title: Wednesday: Week 13 ┬╖ Scaling Databases + MySQL Comparison
tasks: | ЁЯЫа **Technologies** | PostgreSQL read replicas, PgBouncer, `pg_stat_statements` |

| ЁЯУЦ **Concepts** | Read replicas with streaming replication, PgBouncer connection pooling (transaction mode vs session mode), `pg_stat_statements` for slow query analysis, replication lag monitoring |
| ЁЯОп **You Build** | FleetPulse with read replica: write GPS pings to primary, read aggregations from replica. PgBouncer in transaction mode. |

| ЁЯФЧ **Why It Matters** | A single PostgreSQL primary handles ~5,000 connections maximum. PgBouncer lets 100,000 app connections share a pool of 100 database connections. Read replicas distribute read-heavy queries. |
**MySQL vs PostgreSQL ADR**: write a one-page decision record comparing both. Key differences: MySQL uses InnoDB row-level locking vs PostgreSQL MVCC (no lock for reads), MySQL `ENUM` type vs PostgreSQL CHECK constraints, PostgreSQL supports `JSONB`, arrays, custom types. PostgreSQL chosen for this plan because of MVCC, `JSONB`, and better full-text search.

days.title: Thursday: Week 13 ┬╖ Sharding + Partitioning
tasks: | ЁЯЫа **Technologies** | PostgreSQL partitioning, consistent hash router in Go |
| ЁЯУЦ **Concepts** | Horizontal sharding (split rows across databases), vertical sharding (split columns/tables across databases), range vs hash vs list partitioning, partition pruning |
| ЁЯОп **You Build** | FleetPulse GPS pings table partitioned by month (range partitioning). Queries for "last 24 hours" only touch 1тАУ2 partitions. |

| ЁЯФЧ **Why It Matters** | A 5-billion-row GPS pings table with monthly range partitioning makes `WHERE ping_time > NOW() - INTERVAL '24 hours'` access only 1 partition instead of all 5 billion rows. |

days.title: Friday: Week 13 ┬╖ Non-Relational Databases + Database Selection
tasks: | ЁЯЫа **Technologies** | MongoDB, Cassandra overview, Redis, SQLite, Convex, PGVector |
| ЁЯУЦ **Concepts** | Document model vs relational model, wide-column stores, eventual consistency, BASE vs ACID, picking the right database |

| ЁЯФЧ **Why It Matters** | There is no best database. There is a best database for each access pattern. MongoDB for flexible schema, Cassandra for time-series writes at scale, Redis for sub-millisecond cache, PGVector for semantic similarity. |
**Database selection decision tree**:
- Need SQL + ACID? тЖТ PostgreSQL
- Need flexible schema with frequent writes? тЖТ MongoDB
- Need sub-millisecond reads/writes? тЖТ Redis
- Need semantic similarity search? тЖТ PGVector
- Need real-time reactive queries? тЖТ Convex
- Need embedded (no server)? тЖТ SQLite
- Need time-series at scale? тЖТ TimescaleDB or Cassandra

weeks.title: Week 14: Caching Architecture

days.title: Monday: Week 14 ┬╖ Understanding Caching + Cache Patterns
tasks: | ЁЯЫа **Technologies** | Redis, Node.js, Go `go-redis` |
| ЁЯУЦ **Concepts** | Cache-aside (lazy loading), read-through, write-through, write-behind (write-back), cache stampede, thundering herd, TTL jitter, cache warming |
| ЁЯОп **You Build** | FleetPulse driver profile cache using cache-aside pattern. Surge pricing cache with write-through. TTL jitter preventing stampede. |

| ЁЯФЧ **Why It Matters** | The wrong cache pattern causes stale data, double writes, or stampedes. Cache-aside is correct for most use cases. Write-through is correct when reads must always be fresh. |
**Cache patterns**:

**Cache-aside** (most common):
1. App checks cache тЖТ miss тЖТ app queries DB тЖТ app stores in cache тЖТ returns
2. On write: app updates DB, deletes cache entry (or updates it)
**Read-through**: cache sits in front of DB. App only ever talks to cache. Cache fetches from DB on miss.

**Write-through**: on write, app writes to cache AND DB simultaneously. Cache is always up to date.
**Write-behind**: on write, app writes to cache immediately. DB write is asynchronous. Higher performance, risk of data loss on crash.
**Cache stampede** (thundering herd): a popular key expires. 10,000 simultaneous requests all miss the cache and all query the DB simultaneously. Fix: TTL jitter (add random seconds to TTL so keys don't all expire at once), probabilistic early refresh, `singleflight` deduplication.

days.title: Tuesday: Week 14 ┬╖ Redis Deep Dive: All Data Structures
tasks: | ЁЯЫа **Technologies** | Redis, `go-redis/v9`, Lua scripting |
| ЁЯУЦ **Concepts** | String/Hash/List/Set/Sorted Set/Stream data structures with correct use cases, Lua scripts for atomicity, pipelining, pub/sub |
| ЁЯОп **You Build** | Redis-backed rate limiter (Sorted Set + Lua script). Driver online status (Hash). Job queue (List + BLPOP). Leaderboard (Sorted Set). |
| ЁЯФЧ **Why It Matters** | Using String for everything wastes memory. Using the right structure (Hash for objects, Sorted Set for ranked data) uses 10x less memory and is 10x faster. |
| Structure | Use Case | Time Complexity |
| String | Counters, cache values, SETNX locks | O(1) get/set |
| Hash | Objects: `HSET user:123 name Alice age 30` | O(1) per field |
| List | Queues: `LPUSH jobs X; BLPOP jobs 0` | O(1) push/pop from ends |
| Set | Membership, unique visitors: `SADD`, `SISMEMBER` | O(1) add/check |
| Sorted Set | Leaderboards, rate limiting: `ZADD score member` | O(log N) |

| Stream | Append log, consumer groups: `XADD`, `XREADGROUP` | O(log N) append |
**TTL jitter**: `EXPIRE key $(( base + RANDOM % jitter ))`. Without jitter, keys set by a batch job all expire at the same second тАФ 100,000 simultaneous cache misses.

days.title: Wednesday: Week 14 ┬╖ Caching at Different Architecture Levels
tasks: | ЁЯЫа **Technologies** | Cloudflare CDN, Nginx reverse proxy cache, Redis application cache, PostgreSQL query cache |
| ЁЯУЦ **Concepts** | Browser cache (Cache-Control headers), CDN edge cache, reverse proxy cache (Nginx), application cache (Redis), database buffer pool, CPU L1/L2/L3 cache analogy |

| ЁЯОп **You Build** | FleetPulse: Cloudflare caches static assets at edge. Nginx caches API responses for 5 seconds. Redis caches DB query results for 60 seconds. `Cache-Control` headers correct on every response. |

| ЁЯФЧ **Why It Matters** | Each cache layer is 10тАУ100x faster than the next. Browser cache: 0ms. CDN edge: ~5ms. Redis: ~1ms. PostgreSQL: ~10ms. Without layered caching, every user request hits the database. |
**`Cache-Control` headers**:
- `public, max-age=31536000, immutable` тАФ for versioned static assets (hash in filename). Cache forever.
- `public, max-age=300, stale-while-revalidate=60` тАФ for semi-static API responses. CDN caches for 5 minutes, serves stale for 1 minute while refreshing.
- `private, no-cache` тАФ for user-specific data. Browser can cache but must revalidate.
- `no-store` тАФ for sensitive data (auth tokens, banking pages). Never cache.

days.title: Thursday: Friday тАФ Week 14 ┬╖ System Design: URL Shortener + API Rate Limiter
tasks: | ЁЯЫа **Technologies** | Go, Redis, PostgreSQL, consistent hashing |
| ЁЯУЦ **Concepts** | Hash collision handling, redirect caching, base62 encoding, token bucket algorithm, sliding window log, sliding window counter, Redis Lua atomic rate limiter |
| ЁЯОп **You Build** | Full URL shortener service (like bit.ly). Full API rate limiter service тАФ used by all 5 platforms. Both implemented, tested, k6 benchmarked. |

| ЁЯФЧ **Why It Matters** | URL shorteners teach hash collision, caching, and analytics counters. Rate limiters teach atomic operations, sliding windows, and distributed coordination. These are two of the most common system design interview questions. |
**URL Shortener design**:
- Generate 7-character base62 code from a counter (not random тАФ no collision)
- Store `{code тЖТ original_url}` in PostgreSQL + Redis cache
- Redirect: check Redis first (1ms), fall back to PostgreSQL (10ms), write to Redis
- Analytics: `INCR clicks:{code}` in Redis, batch-flush to PostgreSQL hourly
**Rate Limiter design**:
- Token bucket (Go): bucket fills at rate R tokens/second, max capacity B. Request costs 1 token. Burst-friendly.
- Sliding window (Redis Lua): `ZREMRANGEBYSCORE key 0 (now-window)`, `ZCARD key` check, `ZADD key now requestId`, `EXPIRE key window`. Atomic.

weeks.title: Week 15: Async Systems + Message Queues + Kafka

days.title: Monday: Week 15 ┬╖ Message Queues: DLQ + Retry Policies
tasks: | ЁЯЫа **Technologies** | PostgreSQL (`SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED`), Redis List, DungBeetle |
| ЁЯУЦ **Concepts** | At-least-once delivery, idempotency, dead letter queue (DLQ), exponential backoff with jitter, max retry count, poison pill handling |
| ЁЯОп **You Build** | DungBeetle v1.0: jobs automatically retry with exponential backoff. After max retries, moved to DLQ. DLQ dashboard in UI. |

| ЁЯФЧ **Why It Matters** | Without a DLQ, failed jobs that always fail block the queue forever. The DLQ lets you inspect, fix, and replay failed jobs without losing them. |
**Idempotency + retries**: if a job fails after starting (server crash), it will be retried. The job handler must be idempotent тАФ running it twice produces the same result as running it once. Implement with idempotency keys: `INSERT INTO results (job_id, ...) ON CONFLICT (job_id) DO NOTHING`.

days.title: Tuesday: Wednesday тАФ Week 15 ┬╖ Kafka Essentials
tasks: | ЁЯЫа **Technologies** | Apache Kafka, MSK, Confluent Go Kafka client |
| ЁЯУЦ **Concepts** | Topic тЖТ partition тЖТ offset model, consumer groups, partition key selection, idempotent producer, exactly-once semantics (EOS), consumer lag monitoring, outbox pattern |
| ЁЯОп **You Build** | FleetPulse GPS events via Kafka (not direct DB write). DungBeetle consuming from Kafka topic. PayCore payment events via Kafka for analytics. |
| ЁЯФЧ **Why It Matters** | Kafka decouples producers from consumers, buffers traffic spikes, enables event replay, and provides exactly-once delivery for financial operations. |
**Core Kafka concepts**:
- **Topic** тАФ named log of events. Like a database table but append-only.
- **Partition** тАФ a topic is split into N ordered partitions. Each partition is a separate ordered log. Partitions enable parallelism.
- **Offset** тАФ each event in a partition has a monotonically increasing offset number. Consumers track their offset.
- **Consumer group** тАФ N consumers sharing work. Each partition assigned to exactly one consumer in the group. Adding consumers redistributes partitions (rebalance).

- **Partition key** тАФ determines which partition receives a message. Same key тЖТ same partition тЖТ ordering guaranteed for that key. Use `driverId` as key to ensure GPS pings for one driver are ordered.

days.title: Thursday: Week 15 ┬╖ Real-Time PubSub: Redis + WebSockets + SSE
tasks: | ЁЯЫа **Technologies** | Redis pub/sub, WebSockets (`gorilla/websocket`), SSE (`text/event-stream`) |
| ЁЯУЦ **Concepts** | Redis pub/sub for fan-out across replicas, WebSocket upgrade handshake, SSE one-way server push, connection registry in `sync.Map`, presence heartbeat protocol |
| ЁЯОп **You Build** | FleetPulse: driver GPS positions pushed to all dispatcher browsers via WebSocket. Analytics dashboard updates via SSE. Redis pub/sub fans out across 3 server replicas. |

| ЁЯФЧ **Why It Matters** | Without Redis pub/sub, a message sent to replica 1 is only seen by clients connected to replica 1. With pub/sub, all replicas receive it and broadcast to their connected clients. |
**WebSocket vs SSE**:
- **WebSocket** тАФ full-duplex: client and server both send messages. Used for: chat, collaborative editing, driver GPS updates (bidirectional).
- **SSE** тАФ server тЖТ client only. Simpler. Auto-reconnects. Uses regular HTTP. Used for: analytics dashboards, notification feeds, progress updates.

days.title: Friday: Week 15 ┬╖ System Design: Realtime Abuse Masker + Notifications at Scale
tasks: | ЁЯЫа **Technologies** | Go, Kafka, Redis, PostgreSQL, APNs/FCM |
| ЁЯУЦ **Concepts** | Streaming classifier, shadow banning, fan-out on write vs fan-out on read, APNs/FCM push delivery, deduplication, notification preferences |
| ЁЯОп **You Build** | RouteMaster notifications: order status pushed via APNs/FCM + WebSocket. Fan-out implemented. Deduplication via Redis SETNX. |

| ЁЯФЧ **Why It Matters** | Notification fan-out at scale: if a user has 10M followers, publishing a tweet must fan-out to 10M notification queues. Pre-compute (fan-out on write) vs lazy compute (fan-out on read) is the core tradeoff. |
